coredata.dc:title: Ecofriendly nanoprobe for differentiation between hydrogen peroxide producing bacteria and nonproducers: Zero waste circular economy of cooking and harvesting of energy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdella, Aya A.
El-Ekhnawy, Engy
Hammad, Sherin F.
Elmalla, Samah F.
coredata.dc:description: A novel, affordable, and environmentally benign highly fluorescent carbon dots valorized from ignited peel of flame roasted eggplant (EPCDs) were introduced for the first time. The EPCDs were spontaneously and rapidly formed during eggplant roasting, for only 5 min, with high quantum yield (28.5 %). The emission of EPCDs was selectively quenched in the presence of Fe3+ through a mixed static quenching/ inner filter mechanism. Accordingly, a turn-off sensing platform composed of EPCDs coupled to Fe2+ enabled the highly sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) through an acetate mediated Fe2+ oxidation. Acetate could boost sensor sensitivity by blocking Fe2+ back conversion to Fe3+ enabling the rapid detection of H2O2 down to concentration range from 13.12 to 87.55 nM, with very good accuracy (100 % ± 3), precision (%RSD < 3) and low detection limit (3.88 nM). The sensor was applied for H2O2 detection in different water samples as well as for the differentiation of H2O2-producing bacteria from nonproducers, for the first time, in liquid cell culture without any pretreatment step. The utilized EPCDs showed ecological superiority on Complex GAPI and AGREE metrics in addition to a 250-fold reduction in CO2 emission compared to the greenest reported H2O2 sensor paving the way for circular economy practice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sustainable sensor
Carbon dots
Complex GAPI
Hydrogen peroxide
H2O2-producing bacteria

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedures for obtaining CDs from roasted eggplant peels (EPCDs)
General procedure for H2O2 detection using the EPCDs sensor
Preparation, optical properties, and characterization of EPCDs
Optimization of sensor for H2O2 detection
Detection of H2O2 in different water samples
Differentiation between H2O2-producing bacteria and nonproducers

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical properties of EPCDs (a) UV–Vis, excitation, and emission spectra, inset photos of EPCDs solution under white (left) and UV light (365 nm) (right), (b) ATR-FTIR spectrum of the EPCDs showing the surface functionality.

(a) TEM images of EPCDs showing uniform spherical EPCDs, (b) HRTEM images of the EPCDs showing lattice fringe of 0.25 nm (inset lattice fringe measurement), (c) SAED pattern of EPCDs demonstrating its crystallinity, (d) TEM image of the proposed EPCDs/Fe2+ after addition of H2O2 showing aggregated EPCDs.

FL quenching of EPCDs as a function of H2O2 concentration (a) overlay of emission spectra using different H2O2 concentrations (0–300 nM), (b) Linear relationship between degree of quenching (F0/F) and H2O2 concentration within the range of 13.12–87.55 nM.

Detection of H2O2-producing bacteria using the proposed EPCDs/Fe2+ sensor, (a) overlay of emission spectra of EPCDs/Fe3+ before and after addition of 20 μL of H2O2-PB (2 × 107 CFU/mL), (b) The demonstrated selective FL quenching of EPCDs/Fe2+ by H2O2-PB (2 × 107 CFU/mL) in comparison with non H2O2-PB, including Gram-positive and Gram-negative candidates (2 × 107 CFU/mL of each) (Asterisk (*) indicates significant quenching attained by H2O2-PB, ANOVA, p < 0.05), (c) Photos of sensor solutions with different bacteria showing possible visual selective detection of H2O2-PB under UV light (365 nm).
Assessment of EPCDs sensing strategy using complex GAPI and AGREE in comparison with reported bio-sourced CDs sensor [22].
Diagrammatic representation of EPCDs preparation procedures and proposed sensing platform for H2O2 detection.
Regression parameters for H2O2 detection using the proposed EPCDs/Fe2+ sensor at optimum conditions.
Recovery of H2O2 from spiked environmental, tap, and mineral water samples using the proposed EPCDs/Fe2+ sensor.

head.ce:title: Ecofriendly nanoprobe for differentiation between hydrogen peroxide producing bacteria and nonproducers: Zero waste circular economy of cooking and harvesting of energy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, 31111, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology and Immunology, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Microbiology and Immunology, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, 31111, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Elgeish Street, The Medical Campus of Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Medical Campus of Tanta University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel, affordable, and environmentally benign highly fluorescent carbon dots valorized from ignited peel of flame roasted eggplant (EPCDs) were introduced for the first time. The EPCDs were spontaneously and rapidly formed during eggplant roasting, for only 5 min, with high quantum yield (28.5 %). The emission of EPCDs was selectively quenched in the presence of Fe3+ through a mixed static quenching/ inner filter mechanism. Accordingly, a turn-off sensing platform composed of EPCDs coupled to Fe2+ enabled the highly sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) through an acetate mediated Fe2+ oxidation. Acetate could boost sensor sensitivity by blocking Fe2+ back conversion to Fe3+ enabling the rapid detection of H2O2 down to concentration range from 13.12 to 87.55 nM, with very good accuracy (100 % ± 3), precision (%RSD < 3) and low detection limit (3.88 nM). The sensor was applied for H2O2 detection in different water samples as well as for the differentiation of H2O2-producing bacteria from nonproducers, for the first time, in liquid cell culture without any pretreatment step. The utilized EPCDs showed ecological superiority on Complex GAPI and AGREE metrics in addition to a 250-fold reduction in CO2 emission compared to the greenest reported H2O2 sensor paving the way for circular economy practice.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a colorless oxidant belonging to reactive oxygen species that is naturally produced as a byproduct of many biological processes [1]. Moreover, it is considered as environmental pollutant [2] and pathogenic marker [3]. H2O2 is abundant in different water samples. It can be formed naturally by photochemical decomposition of water, added during water treatment to remove excess metals, or produced by certain bacterial contaminants. Its occurrence in environmental water constitutes a huge danger because it undergoes heavy metal catalyzed oxidation forming free radicals. Furthermore, H2O2 production is well known trait of some bacteria including both commensals, such as streptococci and lactobacilli, and pathogenic species, such as Streptococcus mutans, Streptococcus pyogenes, and Streptococcus pneumoniae [4]. H2O2 production is a main mechanism used by gut and vaginal microbiota to suppress the growth of pathogenic microorganisms [5]. Therefore, the sensitive determination of H2O2 levels in water is essential. Also, the differentiation between H2O2-producing bacteria (H2O2-PB) and nonproducers is important for research, toxicological aspects, and diagnostic purposes [6].

From chemical point of view, H2O2 is a highly unstable small molecule with very simple chemical formula that dissociates rapidly into water. It possesses no chromophore and cannot be derivatized which renders its determination highly challenging [7]. Due to the importance of its determination, various methods have been reported for its detection in different samples, including titrimetric [8], colorimetric [9], chemiluminescent [10], amperometric [11], and nanozyme based methods [12]. However, these methods lack the desired sensitivity, therefore they are not suitable for trace detection of H2O2. In addition, most of these methods are time and energy consuming. Since H2O2 is unstable, its fast determination will help increase the reliability of the obtained results.

The differentiation between H2O2-PB and nonproducers is usually performed using specific culture media such as peroxide indicator plates [13], Prussian blue- forming agar [4], and other modified agar media [14]. In such methods, bacteria are grown on specific media for at least 24 h and its ability to form H2O2 is indicated by media discoloration. Such process is time-consuming and requires specific culture media. Moreover, the application of any of the reported H2O2 detection methods for this purpose has never been reported in literature. This can be ascribed to the potential interferences from bacterial cells during measurements. Thus, the development of highly sensitive methods would enable the detection of the formed H2O2 using very small sample volume which diminishes the number of cells in reaction media.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been recognized as one of the most efficient materials that have attracted broad research interest [15] [16]. CDs have enabled the sensitive and rapid detection of nonfluorescent analytes and even those lacking a chromophore [17] [18]. Particularly, CDs derived from biomass have attracted considerable attention because of their low cost, environmental friendliness, and sustainability [19]. Recently, different nanoparticle-based sensors have been reported for detection of H2O2 down to μM and nM levels. The reported H2O2 sensors are considered environmentally hazardous due to the use of hazardous starting material, excessive energy consumption during preparation (8-24 h) [20–22], and dependence on free radical formation [22]. Surprisingly, the spontaneous formation of CDs during food cooking has been reported in literature, where CDs were valorized from sweet potato peels and roasting residues [23,24]. Circular economy and valorization of endogenous CDs can constitute a sustainable and environmentally benign alternative with net-zero energy consumption which meets the sustainability goals to alleviate the world energy crisis and improve resources efficiency [25–27].

In this study, a sustainable sensor was developed for detection of H2O2 to be applied for differentiation between H2O2-PB and nonproducers. This was accomplished through the valorization of CDs from peels of flame roasted eggplants (EPCDs). The developed sensing strategy was successfully applied for H2O2 detection in different water samples with acceptable selectivity. Moreover, the direct application of the developed sensor could efficiently, for the first time, differentiate H2O2-PB from two nonproducers. It is worth noting that the developed strategy required neither specific media nor trained personnel and was accomplished using 20 μL of liquid culture. To the best of our knowledge, the spontaneous formation of carbon dots during flame roasting of eggplant has never been reported nor exploited before. Additionally, the developed sensor showed ecological superiority compared to a reported bio-sourced CDs sensor on both Complementary Green Analytical Procedure Index (Complex GAPI) and Analytical GREEnness (AGREE) metric tools along with a considerable reduction in carbon footprint.

Eggplant (Solanum melongena L. Rondona) was obtained from local market (Tanta, Egypt). H2O2 (50.00 %) was supplied by the Nasr Company for Intermediate Chemicals (Abu Rawash, Egypt). Ferrous sulfate was supplied by Riedel-de Haën (Hannover, Germany). Anhydrous ferric chloride was obtained from Srl chemicals (Mumbai, India). Quinine sulfate was obtained from Alpha Chemika (Mumbai, India). KCl, NaCl, NaHCO3, NaNO3, and Na2SO4 were obtained from Isochem (Vert-le-Petit, France) and were of laboratory reagent grade. Environmental (River) water samples (EW) were collected from Bahr Shebeen Nilotic Canal (Tanta, Egypt), filtered through a 0.45 μm Millipore filter, and stored in refrigerator at 4 °C until further utilized. Tap water samples (TW) were collected from Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry lab, Faculty of pharmacy Tanta University (Tanta, Egypt), and filtered through a 0.45 μm Millipore syringe filter. Natural mineral water (MW) was obtained from local market (Hamburg, Germany).

The utilized bacterial isolates in the current study were Streptococcus mutans (ATCC 25175), Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 29231) as an example for Gram positive bacteria, and Escherichia coli (ATCC 25922) as an example for Gram negative bacteria. Nutrient broth and brain heart infusion agar were obtained from Oxoid (Hampshire, UK).

Fluorescence spectra and UV/Vis spectra were recorded on a Jasco FP-6300 spectrofluorometer and a Jasco V-530 UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with scan speed of 1000 nm/min and slit width adjusted at 5 nm for both excitation and emission using Spectra manager software (version 1.55). A Perkin Elmer Spectrum 100 ATR-FTIR (Utah, USA) was used. Zeta potential measurements were conducted on a Malvern Zetasizer (Cambridge, UK) in a disposable zeta cell. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images, selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED), and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) were conducted using a JEOL (JEM- 2100F) High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (Tokyo, Japan) operated at accelerated voltage of 200 mV. Bransonic ultrasonic cleaner bath 3510 (Danbury, USA), HANNA pH 211 pH-meter with double junction glass membrane electrode (Woonsocket, USA), and Hettich centrifuge (Tuttlingen, Germany) were utilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for obtaining CDs from roasted eggplant peels (EPCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Eggplant was processed, following the ordinary roasting steps, as follows: it was soaked in water for 5 min, washed to remove residual soil, dried, and placed on direct gas stove fire for 5 min/side until the peel is burnt flaky and the pulp is turned soft. Then, it was left to cool down to room temperature. The ignited peel was collected, air dried, then finely powdered using an electric grinder. The ignited black fine powder was stored in dry clean container until further utilized (Fig. S1).

For sensor solution preparation, the EPCDs were extracted by placing 0.1 g of the powdered roasted eggplant peel in 100 mL distilled water. Then, the solution was sonicated for 30 min, centrifuged (5 min, 6000 rpm), and filtered through 0.22 μm syringe filter. Then, the solution was dialyzed against distilled water for 2 h. The EPCDs solution was stable when stored in refrigerator at 4 °C for one month.

The % quantum yield (% QY) of the obtained EPCDs was determined relative to quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) as a reference standard using double distilled water and 0.1 M H2SO4 (η = 1.33) as dilution solvent for EPCDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. The Abs of EPCDs and quinine sulfate was recorded at 340 nm and 350 nm, respectively, and were kept below 0.05 for all measurements to eliminate the scatter effect. The FL intensity of both solutions was recorded and QY was calculated using Eq. 1 [24].
(1)
Q x
=
Q st
×
F x
F st
×
ղ x
ղ st
2
×
A st
A x
where F is the fluorescence intensity, A represents the absorbance value, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscripts “x” and “st” refer to EPCDs and quinine sulfate, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for H2O2 detection using the EPCDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Different volumes from 30 to 600 μL of working standard H2O2 solution (0.875 μM) were added to 400 μL of EPCDs sensor solution, 120 μL of 5.0 mM FeSO4.7H2O, and 700 μL of 0.1 M acetic acid solution. The solution was made up to 2 mL using distilled water. The solution was incubated for 4 min then, FL was recorded at 432 nm after excitation at 340 nm. Calibration curve was constructed by plotting F0/F vs the corresponding H2O2 concentration (nM), where F0 and F are the FL intensity of the EPCDs before and after addition of H2O2.

ce:list-item.ce:para: For H2O2 detection in different water samples: 780 μL of water samples (EW, TW, and MW) spiked at different H2O2 levels were analyzed following the general procedure for H2O2 detection (section 2.5).

For H2O2- PB detection: bacterial suspensions were prepared by suspending the bacterial isolates in nutrient broth overnight at 37 °C for 24 h. Serial dilutions of bacterial suspension were prepared (103–2 × 108 CFU/mL). The number of colony forming units per milliliter (CFU/mL) was calculated by multiplying the total number of colonies, obtained in one milliliter, by the dilution factor [28]. An aliquot of 20 μL of bacterial cell culture (1 × 103 - 2 × 108 CFU/mL) were analyzed directly following the general procedure for H2O2 detection (section 2.5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation, optical properties, and characterization of EPCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since flame roasting of eggplant involves the direct exposure of the peels to high temperature, pyrolysis induced carbonization was supposed to take place (Fig. S1). Therefore, the dried ignited peel was subjected to sonication assisted extraction using water as extraction solvent. The solution was filtered to remove large particles and dialyzed against distilled water to get rid of the small molecular fluorophores (Scheme 1 ). The resulting material was subjected to a set of investigations to confirm carbonization and characterize the formed EPCDs.

The EPCDs solution exhibited high emission at 432 nm after excitation at 340 nm (Fig. 1(a)) with a QY of 28.5 %. Intense blue fluorescence was observed by inspecting the EPCDs solution under UV light (365 nm) indicating the presence of highly fluorescent material in the solution (Fig. 1(a) inset photo). The UV/Vis spectrum of the EPCDs, scanned between 200 and 800 nm, showed no absorbance in the visible region excluding the contribution of peel coloring dyes. Moreover, The UV spectrum of the EPCDs solution (Fig. 1(a)) displayed a broad band at 320 nm which corresponds to n-π* of CO, while π-π* transition of CC was observed at 270 nm. Furthermore, the emission spectra were recorded at different excitation wavelengths showing CDs' characteristic excitation dependent emission (Fig. S2) [29].

The surface functionality of the EPCDs was investigated using FTIR spectroscopy. The recorded FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1(b)) showed OH (3358 cm−1), CO (1678 cm−1), C=C(1412 cm−1), and C-O-C (1065 cm−1) stretches, indicating both OH and COOH surface functional groups [30]. The surface charge of the obtained EPCDs was measured using Zetasizer and was found to be −19.3 mV (Fig. S3 (a)), which confirms negative surface functionality that impart enhanced colloidal stability and possible metal complexation [24].

The XRD pattern (Fig. S3 (b)) showed a diffraction peak localized at 2Theta of 23.06, which corresponds to the graphitic carbon (002) lattice planes [31]. The formed EPCDs were visualized using HRTEM which showed uniform spherical nanoparticles (Fig. 2(a)) with an average size of ∼2.68 ± 1.04 nm and narrow particle size distribution, as shown in Fig. S4. The EPCDs exhibited lattice fringe with a d-spacing of 0.25 nm (Fig. 2(b)), which corresponds to the (002) plane lattice of graphite [32]. This indicates the crystalline nature of the EPCDs which was confirmed by SAED pattern shown in Fig. 2(c). The elemental composition of the EPCDs was determined using EDX-TEM (Fig. S5) revealing atomic composition of C and O that were estimated to be 95.53 % and 4.47 %, respectively. This suggests that EPCDs originated mainly from cellulose of eggplant peel.

H2O2 is a strong oxidizing agent that has been reported to produce hydroxyl radicals especially in the presence of metal catalysts such as iron, where it is oxidized to Fe3+ producing a hydroxyl radical and a hydroxide ion followed by a back reduction step of Fe3+ by another molecule of hydrogen peroxide [33]. As shown in Fig. S6(a), Fe3+could significantly and selectively turn off the FL of EPCDs, while minor quenching effect was observed with Fe2+ and other metals. Upon the addition of H2O2, the emission of EPCDs/Fe2+ sensor was markedly quenched (Fig. S6(b)). Accordingly, a turn off sensing platform was constructed using EPCDs coupled to Fe2+. Nevertheless, the measured response was unstable which could possibly be ascribed to back conversion of Fe3+ to Fe2+ leading to signal loss (Fig. S7). Acetate ion is reported to undergo free radical catalyzed decomposition. Thus, it could eliminate Fe3+ back conversion associated hydroxyl radicals resulting in response enhancement and stabilization [34] (Fig. S7). As shown in Fig. S7, a stable response (F0/F) was attained after 4 min and was in the stasis for 20 min ensuring sensor reliability. The principle of H2O2 determination using the proposed EPCDs/Fe2+ sensor is illustrated in Scheme 1.

The underlying interactions were further investigated using UV/Vis spectroscopy, spectrofluorimetry, and TEM images. Direct interaction between EPCDs and H2O2 was omitted by the identical UV/Vis and emission spectra of EPCDs before and after addition of H2O2, as shown in Fig. S8(a) and (b), respectively. Nevertheless, the addition of H2O2 to EPCDs/ Fe2+ resulted in obvious change in its UV spectrum (Fig. S8(a)) with a noticeable FL quenching indicating probable static quenching of the EPCDs with produced Fe3+. This was assisted by HRTEM micrograph of EPCDs/Fe2+ after addition of H2O2 (Fig. 2(d)) which shows the formed CDs aggregates. The quenching mechanism was further supported by relating the FL quenching to Fe3+ concentration at different temperatures (25, 30, and 35 °C) using Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. 2).
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
Q
where F
0 and F are the FL intensities of EPCDs in the absence and presence of the quencher (Fe3+), respectively, [Q] is the quencher concentration, and K
SV is the Stern-Volmer constant.

The negative effect of temperature on K
SV (Fig. S8 (c)) supported the contribution of static quenching mechanism where a ground state complex is formed between fluorescer (EPCDs) and quencher (Fe3+) [17]. Furthermore, to investigate the potential influence of inner filter effect, the observed FL were corrected using Eq. 3 [35] [36].
(3)
F corrected
=
F observed
× antilog
A ex
+
A em
2
where,
F corrected
,
F observed
refers to the FL after and before the exclusion of IFE, respectively and
A ex
,
A em
denotes Fe3+ absorbance at EPCDs' excitation and emission wavelength, respectively.

The observed and corrected % quenching efficiency (%E) were calculated using Eq. 4 and plotted against the corresponding Fe3+ concentrations (Fig. S8 (d)). The demonstrated minor reduction in quenching efficiency after IFE correction indicates a minor contribution of IFE [36].
(4)
% E =
1 −
F
F 0
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of sensor for H2O2 detection
ce:section.ce:para: As explained in the previous section, back reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ would decrease the intensity of the measured response and hence sensor sensitivity. This back conversion is mainly affected by pH and acetate concentration. Accordingly, the pH of the reaction media was investigated between 1.0 and 8.5. As shown in Fig. S9(a), maximum F0/F ratio attained at pH 3.0 which was consistent with reported pH values for H2O2 mediated Fe2+ oxidation [37]. At pH values higher than 4.0, marked decrease in the measured response was evident which can be attributed to Fe3+ precipitation or its back reduction by hydroxyl radicals [38]. Moreover, acetic acid concentration was adjusted to 0.035 M (Fig. S9(b)). At higher concentration, acetic acid would compete with the EPCDs for Fe3+ complexation and subsequent decrease in quenching efficiency [39].

Additionally, the rate of Fe3+ formation and amount of formed Fe3+ are also affected by concentration of Fe2+. Therefore, Fe2+ concentration was investigated, as shown in Fig. S9(c), and maximum F0/F was obtained using 0.3 μM. Furthermore, the optimum concentration of EPCDs in sensor solution was found to be 0.25 μg/mL (Fig. S9(d)).

The optimized conditions enabled highly sensitive detection of H2O2 with very good linearity (R2 = 0.997) over the concentration range between 13.12 and 87.55 nM, (Fig. 3(a) and (b)). Regression parameters including slope, intercept, and residual standard deviation were calculated and presented in Table 1 . Moreover, limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the formula 3.3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of intercept and S is the slope of calibration curve and was found to be 3.88 nM. The accuracy and precision of the developed sensing strategy was indicated by acceptable % recovery and %RSD, respectively (Table S1).

As presented in Table S2, the proposed sensor showed comparable sensitivity to other reported methods with superiority in simplicity and environmental friendliness. This indicated the potential application of the proposed EPCDs/Fe2+ sensor for detection of H2O2 traces different water samples and cell cultures. Compared with the reported spectrofluorimetric H2O2 sensors, the developed EPCDs/Fe2+ sensing probe has the advantages of being simple and environmentally benign. In addition, it exhibited shorter preparation time, and higher quantum yield utilizing green precursors. Moreover, the obtained EPCDs required neither special equipment nor excessive energy expenditure for their preparation. Furthermore, the proposed sensor was completely obtained from green edible non-toxic plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 in different water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure sensor reliability, it was applied for H2O2 determination in different water samples, spiked at 3 concentration levels. As presented in Table 2 , mean %recovery and RSD were found within 100.00 ± 5.00, and ˂ 3 %, respectively, indicating the ability of the proposed sensor to detect H2O2 in the tested water samples with acceptable accuracy and precision. The obtained results were statistically compared with a reported colorimetric method which is based on the oxidation of Fe2+ followed by subsequent complexation of the formed Fe3+ with thiocyanate to form red colored ferric thiocyanate (measured at 470 nm) [40]. Both F-test and t-test showed no significant differences between results of developed sensor and reference method (p-value >0.05), as shown in Table 2. Moreover, the proposed sensor demonstrated acceptable selectivity when tested against potential interfering ions by recording the FL intensity of EPCDs/Fe2+ sensor solution in presence of some potential interfering cations and anions (Fig. S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation between H2O2-producing bacteria and nonproducers
ce:section.ce:para: The developed EPCDs/Fe2+ sensor was successfully applied to differentiate between a candidate H2O2-PB, S. mutans, from non H2O2-PB candidates, Staphylococcus aureus, and Escherichia coli. Different samples volumes were tried. The high sensitivity of the proposed sensor enabled the spectrofluorimetric detection of the H2O2-PB (S. mutans) using a very small sample volume (20 μL) where larger sample volumes were avoided to avoid matrix interferences (media components and bacteria). As shown in Fig. 4(a), the presence of S. mutans could achieve marked FL quenching. On the other hand, both negative controls showed no significant changes in FL intensity of the sensor solution (ANOVA p > 0.05), as shown by Fig. 4(b). The obtained results demonstrated selectivity of the developed sensor to detect H2O2-PB without any interference from bacteria cells or culture media. This indicates the ability of the proposed sensor to selectively identify H2O2-PB in their liquid cultures without any specific media and in only 4 min (instead of 24 h). Moreover, FL quenching exhibited by the tested H2O2-PB could be visually detected using a simple UV cabinet (Fig. 4(c)). This demonstrated the practical applicability of the proposed strategy in microbiology lab using regular nutrient broth media. Future studies would investigate the EPCDs/Fe2+ as bioimaging agent for confocal laser scanning microscopy to differentiate H2O2-PB from nonproducers as well as to detect antibiotic resistance due to reactive oxygen species production.

In light of sustainability aspects, minimization of energy consumption, hazard reduction, and waste elimination are major features. The greenness of the EPCDs/Fe2+ sensor was compared with a reported bio-derived CDs sensor [22] using both Complex GAPI [42] and AGREE metric tools [41]. Complex GAPI metric has recently been introduced in which a hexagonal field was added to the original GAPI graph to reflect the processes performed prior to the sample preparation step and final analysis such as reagent preparation. Both metric tools showed superior performance of EPCDs/Fe2+ (Fig. 5 ). On AGREE calculator, EPCDs/Fe2+ sensing strategy showed a score of 0.8 compared to 0.62 for the comparison sensing strategy. This can be attributed to the high energy consumption (200 °C for 8 h), special pressure equipment (hydrothermal autoclave), and generation of highly toxic wastes (free radicals) which rendered the reported bio-sourced sensor environmentally hazardous (Fig. 5). Regarding EPCDs preparation, almost no hazard was evident which can be ascribed to the use of edible nontoxic starting material and waste passivation through the implementation of acetate mediated hydroxyl radical reduction. In addition, neither special equipment nor harsh conditions were required. The carbon footprint was calculated for both methods and was found to be 0.0079 and 1.976 Kg CO2 equivalent for EPCDs and reported bio-sourced sensor, respectively. The developed sensor was proved to be an ideal analytical method where CO2 emission is maintained less than 0.1 [43]. In addition to the demonstrated ecological superiority, the presented EPCDs sensor exhibited higher QY (28.5 %) than the reported sensor (10.3 %).

In this work, a novel simple rapid cost effective, and environmentally benign sensing platform was developed for sensitive detection of H2O2. The proposed sensor was constructed using blue emissive CDs valorized from flame roasted eggplant peel ash coupled with Fe2+. The FL of the EPCDs/Fe2+ sensor was quenched upon addition of H2O2 through acetate mediated hydroxyl radicals' elimination. This sensing platform could successfully detect H2O2 at concentration level down to 3.38 nM. This unprecedented high sensitivity enabled the application of the proposed sensor for detection of H2O2 in different water samples, including tap, river, and mineral water. Furthermore, the developed sensing platform could successfully be applied for direct spectrofluorometric, and UV light-aided visual detection of H2O2-PB in liquid culture using regular nutrient broth media. Accordingly, it can be applied for the differentiation of H2O2-PB from other nonproducers which paves the way for routine use of greener alternatives for microbiological lab testing. The developed sensor showed excellent greenness regarding synthetic procedures as indicated by Complex GAPI and AGREE tools with 250-fold reduction in CO2 emissions. Thus, the demonstrated harness of resources as well as harvesting of energy are a considerable milestone towards circular economy practice.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Aya A. Abdella: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Engy El-Ekhnawy: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sherin F. Hammad: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Samah F. Elmalla: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interest or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.susmat.2025.e01265.
